```
Input: [Example Input 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`

title: Count factors of a number

```
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: Iterate from 1 to sqrt(N). If N % i == 0, count both i and N/i.
If i == N/i, count only once.
Time complexity: O(sqrt(N)).

hints: Reverse the number using modulo and division.

```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: Instead of applying each operation directly (O(m*n) per op), accumulate row and column increments.
Use two arrays: rowInc[m] and colInc[n].
Final value at (i,j) = original[i][j] + rowInc[i] + colInc[j].

title: Number of ways to reach destination in a matrix
description:
Given an m x n grid with obstacles, find the number of unique paths from top-left to bottom-right. You can only move down or right. Walmart OA 2025.

**Example 1:**
```
Input: [Example Input]
Output: [Example Output]
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: Use DP. dp[i][j] = number of ways to reach (i, j).
dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1] if cell is not an obstacle.
Base case: dp[0][0] = 1 if not an obstacle.

```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: Use BFS level order traversal. The last node of each level is the right view.
Alternatively, DFS: visit right child before left. Track max depth seen.
If current depth > max depth seen, this node is part of the right view.

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: Split into 3 parts: left boundary (top-down), leaf nodes (left-right), right boundary (bottom-up).
Left boundary: follow left child; if absent, follow right. Don't include leaves.
Leaf nodes: standard DFS, add only leaf nodes.

title: Design LRU/LFU Cache (Walmart LLD Round)
description:

**Example 1:**
```
Input: [Example Input]
Output: [Example Output]
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: LRU: HashMap + Doubly Linked List. Head = most recent, Tail = least recent.
LFU: HashMap + frequency map + Doubly Linked List per frequency.
On get: move to head (LRU) or increment frequency (LFU).
On put: evict from tail (LRU) or evict minimum frequency item (LFU).

title: OOPS - Explain SOLID Principles with examples
description:
Walmart behavioral/theory question. Explain each SOLID principle with a real-world Java example. Walmart Interview Round 2024.

**Example 1:**
```
Input: [Example Input]
Output: [Example Output]
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: S - Single Responsibility: A class should have only one reason to change.
O - Open/Closed: Open for extension, closed for modification. Use interfaces/abstract classes.
L - Liskov Substitution: Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without altering correctness.
I - Interface Segregation: Many specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface.
D - Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions, not concretions.

title: DBMS - Explain Normalization (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF)
description:
Explain database normalization forms with examples. Walmart MCQ/Theory Round 2024.

**Example 1:**
```
Input: [Example Input]
Output: [Example Output]
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: 1NF: Atomic values, no repeating groups. Each cell holds a single value.
2NF: 1NF + no partial dependencies. Every non-key attribute depends on the whole primary key.
3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependencies. Non-key attributes don't depend on other non-key attributes.
BCNF: 3NF + every determinant is a candidate key.

title: OS - Explain Deadlock and its prevention
description:
What is a deadlock? What are the necessary conditions? How to prevent/avoid it? Walmart MCQ/Theory Round 2024-2025.

**Example 1:**
```
Input: [Example Input]
Output: [Example Output]
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: Four necessary conditions (Coffman): Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.
Prevention: Break any one condition. E.g., resource ordering breaks circular wait.
Avoidance: Banker's Algorithm - check if granting a request leads to a safe state.
Detection: Use a wait-for graph. If a cycle exists, deadlock is present.

title: Networking - Explain TCP vs UDP
description:
Compare TCP and UDP protocols. When to use each? Walmart MCQ Round 2024.

**Example 1:**
```
Input: [Example Input]
Output: [Example Output]
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: TCP: Connection-oriented, reliable delivery, ordered, flow control, congestion control. Used for HTTP, FTP, Email.
UDP: Connectionless, unreliable, no ordering guarantee, faster. Used for DNS, video streaming, gaming.
TCP uses 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) to establish connection.
UDP is preferred when speed matters more than reliability.

title: Java Collections - HashMap internals
description:
Explain how HashMap works internally in Java. What happens on collision? How does resizing work? Walmart Java Round 2024.

**Example 1:**
```
Input: [Example Input]
Output: [Example Output]
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: HashMap uses an array of buckets. Key's hashCode() determines the bucket index.
Collision handling: Separate Chaining. Linked list at each bucket. Java 8+: converts to red-black tree when bucket size > 8.
Load factor (default 0.75): when size > capacity * loadFactor, resize to 2x and rehash.
equals() and hashCode() contract: if a.equals(b), then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode().

ThreadPoolExecutor params: corePoolSize, maxPoolSize, keepAliveTime, workQueue, rejectionPolicy.

Rejection policies: AbortPolicy (throw), CallerRunsPolicy (run in caller thread), DiscardPolicy, DiscardOldestPolicy.

title: DBMS - Indexing and its types
description:


**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: Index is a data structure (B-Tree, Hash, etc.) that speeds up data retrieval.
Types: Clustered (one per table, determines physical order), Non-Clustered (separate structure, pointer to data).
B-Tree index: good for range queries. Hash index: good for equality lookups.
Don't index: small tables, columns with high write frequency, columns with low cardinality.

title: OS - Process vs Thread
description:
Differentiate between processes and threads. When to use each? Walmart OS/Theory Round 2024.

**Example 1:**
```
Input: [Example Input]
Output: [Example Output]
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: Process: Independent, own memory space, heavyweight, IPC for communication.
Thread: Shares memory with parent process, lightweight, faster context switch.
Use processes for isolation (security, fault tolerance). Use threads for parallelism within an application.
Java: Thread class or Runnable interface. Threads share heap, each has its own stack.

title: DBMS - ACID Properties with examples
description:
Explain ACID properties in database transactions. Walmart DBMS Round 2024-2025.

**Example 1:**
```
Input: [Example Input]
Output: [Example Output]
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: Atomicity: All or nothing. If any part fails, entire transaction rolls back. (Bank transfer example).
Consistency: DB moves from one valid state to another. Constraints are never violated.
Isolation: Concurrent transactions don't interfere. Isolation levels: Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable.
Durability: Once committed, changes persist even after system failure. Achieved via Write-Ahead Logging (WAL).

title: OOP - Design Patterns (Factory, Singleton, Observer, Strategy)
description:
Explain common design patterns with examples. Walmart LLD/Java Round 2024-2025.

**Example 1:**
```
Input: [Example Input]
Output: [Example Output]
Explanation: [Example Explanation]
```

**Example 2:**
```
Input: [Example Input 2]
Output: [Example Output 2]
```

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= N <= 10^5`
- `Time complexity: O(N)`
hints: Singleton: One instance. Use for logging, config, DB connection pool. Thread-safe: double-checked locking or enum.
Factory: Create objects without specifying the exact class. Use when object creation logic is complex.
Observer: Publish-subscribe. Subject notifies all observers on state change. Use for event systems.
Strategy: Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each, make them interchangeable. Use for sorting, payment methods.
